title: Explorer should instrument functions before executing
description: explore_function() in explorer.rs sends Analyze then immediately loops Execute commands, but never sends an Instrument command. Without instrumentation, the TS frontend (and Go frontend) return empty branch_path arrays, so the explorer never gets branch decision data. Fix: after receiving FunctionAnalysis, send an Instrument command and store the result before entering the execution loop.

title: Core: Explorer/orchestrator setup + generator integration
description: Wire setup/teardown lifecycle into explorer.rs and orchestrator.rs exploration loops. For per_function mode: setup before loop, teardown after. For per_execution mode: setup/teardown around each Execute call. Use generator-aware input generation (generate_inputs_with_custom) instead of generate_random_inputs. Check frontend capabilities before sending setup/generate commands. Integration tests.

title: LLM adapter: OpenAI (GPT)
description:
Implement a SeedOracle adapter for the OpenAI API (GPT-4o, GPT-4.1, etc.).

- Use the OpenAI chat completions API with JSON mode for structured output
- Support API key via SHATTER_OPENAI_API_KEY env var or config file
- Support model selection via llm.openai.model (default: gpt-4o)
- Support custom base URL for OpenAI-compatible proxies (llm.openai.base_url)
- Map generic SeedOracle parameters (temperature, max_tokens, timeout) to OpenAI API fields
- Handle rate limiting with exponential backoff
- Handle API errors gracefully (auth failures, quota exceeded, model not found)

title: Design specification output format
description: Define the concrete output artifact for behavior specifications. Each function gets a spec with: (1) behavioral equivalence classes grouped by branch path, (2) preconditions for each class derived from constraints, (3) postconditions (return value pattern, side effects), (4) at least one concrete example per class, (5) distinction between proven properties (Z3-confirmed) and observed properties (seen in all samples but not formally verified). The format must be both human-readable (markdown) and machine-readable (JSON). This is the core deliverable users interact with — build backward from this.

title: Scan orchestrator: drive exploration in dependency order
close_reason: Implemented scan orchestrator module, CLI command, and walkthrough step. All 157 tests pass, clippy clean.

title: fix the clang hacks needed to support z3-sys
description:
The z3-sys crate uses bindgen to generate Rust FFI bindings for Z3, which requires libclang at build time. This caused the devcontainer build to fail with 'Unable to find libclang'.

## What was done (commit ddc41c8)

### Devcontainer fix
- Added `libclang-dev` to `.devcontainer/Dockerfile` apt packages. This provides both `libclang.so` and clang's resource directory (builtin headers like `stdbool.h`) inside the container.

### Local dev fallback
- Added `.cargo/config.toml` with `BINDGEN_EXTRA_CLANG_ARGS` set to `-I/usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/13/include` (with `force = false`). This tells bindgen to use GCC's builtin headers when only `libclang1-18` (runtime .so) is installed without the full `libclang-dev` package. The `force = false` means it's a no-op when `libclang-dev` is installed or the env var is already set.

### Go version fix
- Fixed Go version from 1.25.7 (never released — Go went 1.24 → 1.26) to 1.24.4 in both `.devcontainer/Dockerfile` and `shatter-go/go.mod`.

### Documentation
- Added Prerequisites section to `CLAUDE.md` documenting the `libclang` requirement and how to install it.

## Remaining concern
- The GCC include path in `.cargo/config.toml` is hardcoded to GCC 13 on x86_64. Developers with a different GCC version need to update it manually (`gcc -print-file-name=include` to find the right path).

title: Implement symbolic instrumentation in Go frontend
description: Implement code instrumentation in shatter-go that rewrites Go source to inject branch recording callbacks and symbolic constraint capture. Uses go/ast rewriting. The instrumented code must record branch decisions and path constraints, and be compilable+executable. Parallel to the TS instrumentation work.
close_reason: Instrumentation fully implemented: AST transformation (if/else/switch/for/range), symbolic constraint extraction, runtime recorder, executor with harness generation, and protocol integration. All 29 instrument tests + protocol tests pass. go vet clean.

title: Protocol: Add Setup, Teardown, Generate commands to core protocol
description: Add Setup, Teardown, Generate variants to Command enum and corresponding response variants (Setup, TeardownAck, Generate) to ResponseResult enum in shatter-core/src/protocol.rs. Add optional setup_context field to existing Execute command. Add round-trip serialization tests for all new message types. Backward-compatible: setup_context defaults to None.

title: Support recursive functions in compositional testing
description:
Currently CallGraph::test_order() rejects cycles (including self-recursion) with CallGraphError::Cycle. This means recursive functions cannot be tested compositionally. We need to handle self-recursion and mutual recursion as special cases of compositional testing using iterative deepening with behavior map bootstrapping.

## Strategy

1. Detect self-recursive and mutually-recursive functions from the call graph
2. Identify base-case branches (branches that don't contain recursive calls)
3. Solve for base-case inputs first to build an initial BehaviorMap
4. Mock self-calls using the BehaviorMap built so far, iteratively deepening
5. Stop when coverage stabilizes or max depth is reached

## Edge cases

- No obvious base case (always self-calls) — need max-depth cutoff as safety net
- Base case depends on external state — fall back to random probing
- Higher-order recursion (function passed as argument calls the original) — out of scope initially

## Subtasks

- Detect self-recursive functions and identify base-case branches
- Small-value probing for quick base-case discovery
- Iterative deepening via self-call mocking
- Mutual recursion support
close_reason: All subtasks complete: self-recursion detection, small-value probing, iterative deepening, and mutual recursion support. 168 tests pass, clippy clean.

title: Single-command repo exploration: shatter run <repo-url>
description: Add a top-level CLI command that takes a repo URL (or local path), clones it if needed, discovers all supported files (TS, Go), runs the full pipeline (analyze → explore → report), and outputs a specification report. This is the 'just give me a URL' UX goal. Steps: clone/validate repo, discover files by language, batch analyze all files, build call graph, run concolic exploration (respecting dependency order), generate markdown specs, output combined report. Should support --output-dir for writing per-function spec files.

title: Make Go frontend execution timeout configurable via CLI
description: The Go frontend hardcodes a 10s execution timeout in executor.go (context.WithTimeout 10*time.Second) and a 30s build timeout. These should be configurable from the CLI. Requires: (1) a --exec-timeout flag on the CLI (default 10s), (2) a mechanism to pass the value to the Go frontend — either via subprocess args/env var at spawn time, or via a protocol config message after handshake, (3) executor.go updated to use the configured value instead of the hardcoded 10s. Design decision needed on the transport mechanism (env var is simplest, protocol message is cleanest).

title: Markdown report and progress reporting
description: Generate a human-readable markdown summary alongside the JSON report. Include: function list with coverage indicators, uncovered branch highlights, interesting discovered inputs. For progress: emit to stderr for humans, structured JSON events to stdout when --progress=json for agents. Show current function, progress fraction, and ETA.

title: Rust frontend: shatter-rust
description: Epic: Add a Rust language frontend so Shatter can perform concolic execution on Rust targets. This enables self-hosting (testing shatter-core against itself). Follows the same architecture as shatter-ts and shatter-go: a compiled binary implementing the JSON-over-stdio protocol, with analyze/instrument/execute capabilities for Rust source code.

title: Add .shatter/ configuration to examples as realistic project templates
description:
## Context

Depends on str-9js (candidate inputs and .shatter/ convention). Once the convention is implemented, the examples should demonstrate it.

## Problem

The current examples in `examples/typescript/` and `examples/go/` are flat collections of standalone functions. They don't resemble real projects — no package structure, no configuration, no candidate inputs. Users looking at the examples won't see how to set up .shatter/ for their own projects.

## What to Do

### Restructure examples as realistic mini-projects

Each example language directory should look like a small real project:

```
examples/
  typescript/
    package.json
    tsconfig.json
    .shatter/
      config.yaml               # project-level defaults + per-function overrides
      inputs/
        classifyNumber/
          candidates.json       # known edge cases for this function
        validateToken/
          candidates.json       # realistic auth payloads
        global/
          candidates.json       # common boundary values
    src/
      01-arithmetic.ts
      02-strings.ts
      ...
  go/
    go.mod
    .shatter/
      config.yaml
      inputs/
        ClassifyNumber/
          candidates.json
    ...
```

### Config examples should demonstrate

- Global defaults with reasonable values
- Per-function overrides showing both higher limits (complex functions) and lower limits (simple ones)
- `skip: true` for at least one function
- Candidate inputs that include: typical values, boundary values, regression cases (labeled)
- A glob pattern override (e.g., `"*_test.go":` skip)

### Candidate input examples should demonstrate

- Inputs matching each function's actual parameter types
- Labels explaining why each candidate matters ("boundary: zero", "regression: overflow", "production: typical payload")
- Both function-scoped and global candidates

## Acceptance Criteria

- Each example language has a `.shatter/config.yaml` and at least one `inputs/` directory
- `shatter run examples/typescript/` picks up the config and candidates automatically
- The examples serve as copy-paste templates for users setting up their own projects
- README or comments in config.yaml explain the convention for new users

title: Wire invariant detection into pipeline
description: The invariants.rs module exists but is not wired into the exploration pipeline. Implement Daikon-style invariant detection: after collecting execution records for a function, derive candidate properties (e.g. 'for all x > 100, shipping is free', 'return value is always non-negative when input is positive'). Use proptest to validate candidate invariants against the observed execution set. Properties should be classified as: (1) proven (Z3-confirmed necessary and sufficient), (2) observed (held for all samples, not formally verified), (3) likely (held for >95% of samples). Output feeds into the specification format.

title: Sampling mode: --core-sample and --stratum for representative subset exploration
description:
Add two complementary sampling modes to shatter scan for exploring a representative subset of functions without running the full codebase.

## Two slicing modes

### --core-sample N% (vertical slice)
Selects a thin, representative slice through ALL layers of the call graph. Like a geological core sample — every dependency depth is represented proportionally.

  shatter scan <dir> --core-sample 10%             # 10% of functions, from every layer
  shatter scan <dir> --core-sample 50              # exactly 50 functions, from every layer
  shatter scan <dir> --core-sample 10% --seed 42   # reproducible selection
  shatter scan <dir> --core-sample 10% --batch next  # progressive: next disjoint 10%

**Stratification axes** (each batch is independently representative):
1. Module/directory — proportional representation from each directory subtree
2. Complexity tier — bucket by branch count (0-1, 2-5, 6-15, 16+), sample proportionally
3. Dependency depth — sample from each call graph layer (leaves through top-level callers)
4. Function kind — pure, I/O, constructors, handlers if distinguishable

**Selection algorithm:**
1. Full discovery + batch analyze (cheap — ~1.3s startup + ~50ms/file)
2. Classify functions into strata along axes above
3. Allocate budget proportionally to strata sizes (min 1 per non-empty stratum)
4. Within each stratum, sort by stable hash of (file_path + function_name + seed), pick first N
5. Dependency closure: if selected F calls project function G, include G too (doesn't count against budget)

**Determinism:**
- Default seed = hash(project_name + git HEAD) — same code = same sample
- --seed for explicit override
- Stable under additions: new files don't reshuffle existing selections (consistent hashing)

**Progressive batching (--batch):**
- Functions assigned batch numbers via round-robin within strata
- Each batch is disjoint and independently representative
- Later batches inherit behavior maps from earlier batches (better mocking quality)
- --batch 0 (default), --batch 1, --batch next (auto-detect), --batch 0-2 (run 3 batches)

### --stratum SPEC (horizontal slice)
Explores functions at specific call graph depths. Layer 0 = leaf functions (no internal deps), higher numbers = more callers. Negative indices count from the top of the call graph: -0 = entry points (not called by anything in scope), -1 = second from top, etc.

Uses double-dot range syntax (like git diff) to avoid ambiguity with negative indices.

**Single stratum:**
  --stratum 0          # leaves only (absolute)
  --stratum 3          # depth 3 only (absolute)
  --stratum -0         # top layer (entry points, uncalled within scope)
  --stratum -1         # second from top

**Ranges (double-dot, both endpoints inclusive):**
  --stratum 0..3       # depth 0 through 3
  --stratum -2..-0     # top 3 layers
  --stratum 2..-1      # absolute depth 2 up to second-from-top (mixed)

**Open-ended ranges:**
  --stratum 3..        # depth 3 and everything above
  --stratum ..3        # depth 0 through 3
  --stratum -2..       # second-from-top through top (same as -2..-0)
  --stratum ..-1       # everything except the top layer

**Negative index semantics:**
If the call graph has max depth D, then:
  -0 = D (top), -1 = D-1, -2 = D-2, etc.
This mirrors Python-style indexing (except -0 is the last, not first, since 'top of call graph' is the natural meaning of -0).

**Composable with --core-sample:**
  shatter scan <dir> --stratum 0..2 --core-sample 30%   # 30% of layers 0-2
  shatter scan <dir> --stratum -0 --core-sample 50%     # half the entry points

**Cross-stratum dependency resolution:**
When a function in the selected strata calls a function OUTSIDE the selected strata, the callee is mocked, never pulled in. Mock fidelity depends on prior exploration:

| Scenario | Mock source |
|---|---|
| Callee explored in prior run (cached) | Cached behavior map — high fidelity |
| Callee not yet explored | Type-aware stub — correct types, synthetic values |
| Callee is in the selected strata | Explored naturally (bottom-up), behavior map available |

**Progressive stratum workflow:**
  shatter scan <dir> --stratum 0         # explore leaves, build behavior maps
  # user analyzes
  shatter scan <dir> --stratum 1         # depth-1 gets real mocks from stratum 0
  # user analyzes
  shatter scan <dir> --stratum 2         # even better mocks from strata 0+1

Each layer benefits from behavior maps built by prior layers. Report shows mock quality:
  Stratum 5: 23 functions explored
    Mocking: 41 internal calls
      34 via cached behavior maps (from prior strata)
      7 via type-aware stubs (callees not yet explored)

Within a multi-stratum range, functions are always explored bottom-up (lower strata first).
Cycle groups are assigned to the lowest stratum of any member.

## Output
- Report indicates sampling: 'Explored 47/463 functions (10.2% core sample, strata 0..4)'
- Lists selected functions, stratum assignments, mock quality per function
- --dry-run with --core-sample/--stratum shows selection without exploring
- Cumulative stats across progressive batches

## Edge cases
- --core-sample budget > total functions: scan everything
- Empty strata: redistribute budget
- Dependency closure may push explored count above sample budget — report both
- --stratum N where N > max depth: error with valid range and suggestion
- --stratum -N where N > max depth: clamp to 0
- Resolved range is empty (e.g. 5..3): error
- Range endpoints equal (e.g. 3..3): single stratum, same as --stratum 3

title: Add --request-timeout CLI flag for per-request frontend timeout
description: Currently the per-request timeout (how long the core waits for a single frontend response) defaults to 30s in FrontendConfig::new() and the CLI just reuses --timeout for both the overall deadline and request_timeout. Add a dedicated --request-timeout flag (default 30s) to all commands (explore, scan, run, export-tests) so users can control per-request limits independently from the overall timeout. Wire it through frontend_config() separately from the existing --timeout.

title: Implement file discovery with glob/ignore support
description: Add a discovery module to shatter-core that recursively walks a directory, filters files by language extension (.ts, .go), respects .gitignore, and supports a .shatterignore file for additional exclusions. Sensible defaults: skip node_modules/, vendor/, dist/, target/, __tests__/, and test files (*.test.ts, *_test.go). Support --include and --exclude glob overrides from CLI. Returns a list of (file_path, language) tuples.

title: Add log-level verbosity to CLI and restructure explore output
description:
## Problem

Running `demo/walkthrough.sh` produces excessive output because every individual protocol message (JSON request/response between core and frontend) is dumped to stderr. The signal-to-noise ratio is terrible — hundreds of lines of raw JSON protocol traffic bury the actual useful results.

## Desired Output

The default (INFO-level) output for `shatter explore` should be a concise, human-readable summary per function:

```
safeDivide (examples/typescript/src/04-errors.ts:10)
  3 distinct paths

  Path clusters:
    1. Normal division (b ≠ 0) → returns a/b
       e.g. safeDivide(10.0, 2.0) → 5.0
            safeDivide(-699.9, -1.0) → 699.9
    2. Division by zero (b = 0) → throws Error('division by zero')
       e.g. safeDivide(396.1, 0.0) → Error
            safeDivide(-47.0, 0.0) → Error
    3. Edge: NaN input → returns NaN
       e.g. safeDivide(NaN, 1.0) → NaN
```

Key elements:
- **Function name + source location** (file:line)
- **Number of distinct paths** through the function
- **Path clusters** described as specifications (constraint description → outcome)
- **Highlighted examples** per cluster (2-3 concrete input→output pairs)

## Log Levels

Introduce a `--log-level` / `-v` flag with these tiers:

| Level | What's shown |
|-------|-------------|
| ERROR | Only errors |
| WARN  | Errors + warnings |
| INFO  | Default: function summaries with path clusters and examples (as above) |
| DEBUG | INFO + frontend lifecycle (connect/disconnect, handshake, analysis summary) |
| TRACE | DEBUG + every individual protocol message (current behavior) |

### Console-friendly format

This is a CLI tool, not a service daemon. Log output must be appropriate for interactive console use:

- **No timestamps** — the user is watching it live, timestamps are noise
- **No log-level prefixes** like `[INFO]` or `[DEBUG]` at INFO level — the default output should read like natural tool output, not structured log lines
- At DEBUG/TRACE levels, a short prefix is acceptable (e.g. `[debug]` or `[trace]`) to distinguish diagnostic lines from normal output
- **Color-aware** — use terminal colors when stdout is a TTY (bold for function names, dim for examples, etc.), plain text otherwise
- **No JSON** in default output — the whole point is replacing the raw protocol dump with human-readable summaries

## Performance Data

Add a `--perf` flag (disabled by default) that appends per-function performance stats to the output:

```
safeDivide (examples/typescript/src/04-errors.ts:10)
  3 distinct paths
  ...
  Performance: 100 iterations, 12.3ms total (0.12ms/iter), 5.4KB peak heap
```

This is a separate flag from log level — you can combine `--perf` with any log level. The performance data includes iteration count, wall time, per-iteration average, and peak memory from the frontend's performance reports.

## Where Changes Are Needed

1. **CLI flags**: Add `--log-level` / `-v` / `-q` and `--perf` to clap args in `shatter-cli/src/main.rs`
2. **Frontend stderr logging**: The `[shatter-ts]`, `[shatter-go]`, `[shatter-rust]` prefixed lines in each frontend's handler need to be gated behind a log level (passed via env var or protocol handshake). Currently they unconditionally write to stderr:
   - `shatter-ts/src/main.ts:15` — `log()` function
   - `shatter-go/protocol/handler.go:324` — `logf()` function  
   - `shatter-rust/src/handler.rs:154` — `logf()` function
3. **Exploration report**: Restructure `shatter-core/src/explorer.rs:format_exploration_report()` to produce the clustered summary format described above
4. **CLI explore handler**: `shatter-cli/src/main.rs:260-400` (`run_explore()`) — gate existing print statements by log level
5. **Walkthrough script**: Update `demo/walkthrough.sh` to run at INFO level (the default), so its output demonstrates the clean summary format rather than protocol spam

## Implementation Notes

- Consider using the `log` + `env_logger` crates (or just a simple enum) for the Rust side
- Frontend log level can be communicated via an environment variable (`SHATTER_LOG_LEVEL`) set when spawning the subprocess, or via a field in the handshake message
- The path clustering and specification generation is the hardest part — may need a separate sub-issue for the cluster summarizer logic
- The explore report formatter already exists at `explorer::format_exploration_report()` — it needs to be enhanced, not replaced
- `--perf` is orthogonal to `--log-level` — it controls whether a performance summary line is appended per function, regardless of verbosity

title: Small-value probing for quick base-case discovery
description:
Add a lightweight heuristic that probes recursive functions with boundary values to quickly find base-case inputs before doing any symbolic analysis.

## Rationale

Base cases in recursive functions almost always guard against 'empty' or 'minimal' inputs. Probing with a small set of canonical values is cheap and hits base cases in the vast majority of real recursive functions.

## Implementation

1. Define a set of canonical probe values per type: numbers {0, 1, -1}, strings {"", "a"}, arrays {[], [x]}, booleans {false, true}, null/undefined.
2. When a function is identified as self-recursive, before symbolic solving, run it with probe values (combinatorially for multi-arg functions, but capped at a small budget like 20 executions).
3. Executions that terminate without a recursive call are base-case witnesses. Record them as the initial BehaviorMap entries.
4. This runs as an optional fast pass before the constraint-solving path. If it finds base cases, downstream iterative deepening can start immediately.

## Heuristic: Argument-size monotonicity

As a refinement, detect which argument decreases in the recursive call (n-1, arr.slice(1), node.left, etc.). If detectable, pick probe values that are 'small' in that dimension specifically. This further increases hit rate.

## Key files

- shatter-core/src/explorer.rs — RandomExplorer or new ProbeExplorer
- shatter-core/src/behavior.rs — BehaviorMap::from_records()

## Tests

- Probing factorial(n) with {0, 1, -1} finds base cases n=0 and n=1
- Probing fibonacci(n) with small values finds the base cases
- Probing a function with array argument using [] finds the empty-list base case
- Multi-argument recursive function: probing stays within execution budget
close_reason: Implemented probe_values() and generate_probe_inputs() for small-value probing. Covers canonical boundary values per type (0/1/-1 for ints, empty for strings/arrays, null for nullable). Combinatorial inputs capped at configurable budget.

title: Codebase scan mode: explore all functions in a project
description: A shatter scan <directory> command that discovers all functions in a codebase, orders them by dependency depth, and explores them bottom-up using concolic execution. Results from leaf functions become behavior maps used to mock those functions when testing callers. Supports both TS and Go frontends, multi-process parallelism, sensible auto-mocking defaults, and machine-readable output for agent consumption.

title: Integration tests: shatter explore on Rust targets
description: Write integration tests that run the full shatter explore pipeline against the Rust example functions. Verify: (1) all documented branches are discovered, (2) Z3-generated inputs hit the expected paths, (3) return values match expected outputs for each path, (4) branch coverage reaches 100%% on simple examples, (5) mock injection works for functions with external deps. Follow the same pattern as existing TS integration tests. Check in regression snapshots of exploration output.

title: Epic: Regression testing
description: Snapshot comparison, executable test export (Jest), CI-friendly output. Enables using shatter results to catch regressions.

title: JSON snapshot export and comparison
description: Implement snapshot export: serialize BehaviorClusters to a stable JSON format with function ID, behaviors, exemplar inputs/outputs, and invariants. Implement shatter diff: load a previous snapshot, re-run exploration, compare behaviors. Report matches, regressions (changed behavior), and new behaviors. Include tests with synthetic before/after snapshots.
acceptance_criteria: shatter diff snapshots/test.json correctly identifies matching behaviors, regressions, and new behaviors. Exit code is 0 for all-match, nonzero for regressions.

title: Jest test export
description: Implement shatter export-tests --framework jest: generate a Jest test file from behavior clusters. Each behavior becomes a describe/it block with the exemplar input and expected output/error. Include mock setup code for external dependencies. Generated tests should be directly runnable with npx jest.
acceptance_criteria: Generated test file for a simple function compiles and passes when run with jest. Tests cover both success and error behaviors.

title: Epic: Reporting and specification
description: Behavior clustering, Daikon-style invariant detection, markdown spec generation, and JSON snapshot export. Makes results useful to humans and agents.

title: Behavior clustering by branch path
description: Implement clustering.rs: group ExecutionRecords into BehaviorClusters based on their branch path (sequence of taken/not-taken decisions). Two executions with the same branch path belong to the same cluster. Compute cluster statistics: specimen count, input ranges, output ranges. Include tests with known execution records.
acceptance_criteria: Given 10 execution records for a 2-branch function, correctly groups them into up to 4 clusters (TT, TF, FT, FF). Cluster statistics are accurate.

title: Basic invariant detection
description: Implement invariants.rs: given a BehaviorCluster's specimens, check invariant templates against all specimens. Start with simple templates: numeric comparisons (x > 0, x == C), null checks (x \!= null), string length (s.len() > 0), output-input relations (output == input.field). Use rayon for parallel checking. Filter out trivially true invariants. Include tests with synthetic execution records.
acceptance_criteria: Given specimens where all inputs have x > 0 and output.len == input.len, detects both invariants. Does not report trivially true invariants like x is a number.

title: Markdown specification report generation
description: Implement reporter.rs: given a set of BehaviorClusters with invariants, generate the markdown specification format described in PLAN.md. Include function signature, parameter descriptions with tested ranges, one section per behavior with When/Returns/Throws/Invariant/Performance fields, edge cases section, and dependencies section. Include tests comparing generated output against expected markdown.
acceptance_criteria: Generated markdown for a known function matches the expected format. Output is parseable and readable by both humans and agents.

title: Epic: Packaging and distribution
description: Build pipeline, cross-platform binaries, installation method, frontend bundling. Users need to be able to install and run shatter easily.

title: Packaging: cross-platform Rust binary builds
description: Set up GitHub Actions CI to build shatter-cli for Linux (x86_64, aarch64), macOS (x86_64, aarch64), and Windows (x86_64). Use cross for cross-compilation. Produce static binaries. Include Z3 as a statically-linked dependency. Verify binaries run on target platforms.
acceptance_criteria: CI produces 5 platform binaries. Each binary runs shatter --help successfully on its target platform.
close_reason: Implemented cross-platform release binary CI workflow with GitHub Actions matrix build for 5 targets (Linux x86_64/aarch64, macOS x86_64/aarch64, Windows x86_64). Merged to main.

title: Packaging: bundle TS frontend with core
description: Bundle the TypeScript frontend so it ships with the shatter binary. Options: (1) embed the compiled JS as a resource in the Rust binary and extract at runtime, (2) ship as a companion directory alongside the binary, (3) use bun compile to produce a single-file TS frontend binary. The user should not need to run npm install separately. Include an installation test.
acceptance_criteria: A fresh install of shatter (just the binary + bundled frontend) can run shatter explore on a TypeScript file without any additional setup beyond having Node.js installed.

title: Packaging: installation script and docs
description: Create an installation script (install.sh) that downloads the correct platform binary and installs it to a standard location. Add a --version flag to the CLI. Write a README.md section covering installation, quickstart, and basic usage. Consider Homebrew formula and/or cargo install support.
acceptance_criteria: curl -sSL .../install.sh installs shatter. shatter --version prints the version. README covers installation and a 3-command quickstart.

title: Packaging: bundle Go frontend
description: Bundle the Go frontend binary alongside the shatter core binary. Cross-compile the Go frontend for the same platforms as the core. Include in the installation package.
acceptance_criteria: A fresh install of shatter can run shatter explore on Go files without separately installing the Go frontend.

title: path_hash fallback treats every distinct return value as a unique path
description: When branch_path is empty, path_hash() in explorer.rs falls back to hashing the serialized return value. For numeric functions like safeDivide, every different input pair produces a different float result, so 100 iterations yield 68 'unique paths' through a 4-path function. Fix: use lines_executed as the fallback path signature instead of return value. All executions through the same code path hit the same lines regardless of input values.

title: Example: Add setup + generator usage to example code and walkthrough
description: Create .shatter/setup/ and .shatter/generators/ files for the TypeScript examples (e.g., a setup that initializes some state, generators for domain types like Email or Token). Update examples/typescript/.shatter/config.yaml to reference them. Update demo/walkthrough.sh to exercise setup and generator functionality. This serves as a living integration test and documentation.

title: Behavior map accumulation and composition
description: After exploring a function, distill its results into a behavior map: a mapping from input patterns to output values and side effects. When exploring a caller, inject these behavior maps as mocks for the callee — so the caller is tested with realistic (discovered) behavior rather than arbitrary stubs. Store behavior maps in .shatter-cache/ for reuse across runs.

title: Core: Generator-aware input generation
description: Add ValueSource enum (CustomGenerator | BuiltIn) to input_gen.rs. Add resolve_value_sources() that determines source for each param based on param_generators (name match) then type_generators (type_name match). Add generate_inputs_with_custom() that draws from prefetched custom values or falls back to built-in. Add prefetch_custom_values() that batches Generate protocol commands to frontend. Unit tests with mock value sources.

title: Optional LLM integration for seed generation and stuck-state recovery
description:
Add an optional LLM oracle that augments the concolic engine by generating candidate inputs where symbolic execution struggles. The LLM operates as a seed oracle in two phases: (1) cold-start seed generation before the concolic loop, and (2) stuck-state recovery when the solver fails to find new coverage for K rounds.

## Where LLM adds value over Z3
- Opaque constraints (hashes, checksums, encrypted tokens)
- String/format validation (regex, email, URL, JSON structure)
- Domain-specific seeds (protocol messages, API payloads)
- Loop bound heuristics (suggest meaningful array lengths)
- Cold-start seed quality (structurally valid inputs from function signatures and names)

## Architecture
- New optional SeedOracle trait in shatter-core
- Pluggable backend: implementations provided by separate adapter crates/modules
- Invoked before concolic loop (cold-start) and between rounds (stuck-state)
- LLM receives: function source, parameter types, uncovered branch conditions, failed constraints
- LLM returns: candidate input JSON values, parsed and validated before use

## Recommended configuration parameters
- llm.enabled (bool) — master toggle, default false
- llm.cold_start_candidates (u32) — number of seed inputs to request at cold-start, default 5
- llm.stuck_candidates (u32) — number of inputs to request per stuck-state query, default 3
- llm.stuck_threshold (u32) — rounds with no new coverage before triggering stuck-state query, default 5
- llm.max_queries_per_function (u32) — budget cap to control cost, default 10
- llm.max_tokens_per_query (u32) — output token limit per LLM request, default 1024
- llm.temperature (f64) — sampling temperature, higher for more diverse candidates, default 0.7
- llm.timeout_seconds (u32) — per-request timeout, default 30
- llm.context_lines (u32) — lines of source context to send around the target function, default 50

## Output parsing and validation
- LLM output must be parsed as JSON array of candidate input objects
- Validate each candidate against the function's parameter types (reject type mismatches)
- Deduplicate against existing candidate pool and previously tested inputs
- Handle malformed LLM output gracefully: log warning, skip bad candidates, continue
- Retry with simplified prompt on parse failure (up to llm.max_retries, default 2)
- Structured output prompting preferred where the LLM API supports it (JSON mode, tool_use, etc.)

## Non-goals
- LLM does NOT replace the concolic engine or Z3 solver
- LLM is never in the hot path — only invoked at phase boundaries
- No LLM-based constraint solving (that's Z3's job)

title: Epic: Mocking and compositional testing
description: Automatic dependency detection, mock generation from types, scope configuration, and compositional testing (using results from testing B to mock B when testing A).

title: TS frontend: automatic dependency detection
description: Extend the TS analyzer to detect external dependencies: for each call expression in the target function, trace the symbol to its declaration. If the declaration is outside the target file/scope, report it as an ExternalDependency with symbol name, source module, return type, and call sites. Include tests with functions that import and call external modules.
acceptance_criteria: For a function that calls fetchUser from ./userService, the analyzer reports the dependency with correct symbol, module path, and return type.

title: TS frontend: mock injection via import rewriting
description: Extend the instrumentor to rewrite imports for external dependencies: replace real imports with mock registry lookups. The mock registry is populated by the core before execution based on ExternalDependency analysis. Record all calls to mocked functions (arguments, return values). Include tests demonstrating mock injection and call recording.
acceptance_criteria: A function calling an external fetchUser can be executed with a mock that returns a canned value. The call arguments are recorded and reported back to the core.

title: Scope configuration (shatter.scope.yaml)
description: Implement scope configuration parsing: read shatter.scope.yaml with include/exclude/mock/passthrough globs. The core uses this to tell frontends which dependencies to mock and which to leave as real code. Include the --scope CLI flag. Include tests for glob matching.
acceptance_criteria: Given a scope config that mocks node_modules/** and passes through lodash, the core correctly classifies dependencies. CLI accepts --scope flag.
close_reason: Implemented ScopeConfig, ScopeMatcher, --scope CLI flag, tests (101 pass), walkthrough step, and example file

title: Compositional testing: behavior maps
description: Implement compositional testing: when the core has already tested function B, store its behavior map (inputs -> outputs). When testing function A that calls B, instruct the frontend to mock B using the behavior map. Track which of B's behaviors A actually triggers. Implement call graph ordering (test leaf functions first). Include tests with a two-function dependency chain.
acceptance_criteria: Testing function A that calls function B uses B's recorded behavior as a mock. The core reports which behaviors of B were exercised by A.
close_reason: Implemented BehaviorMap, CallGraph, BehaviorCoverage, and CompositeResult types in behavior.rs with 10 tests including full two-function chain integration test

title: Config: Add setup and generator fields to config structs
description: Add setup, setup_mode, generators, param_generators fields to ShatterConfig, DefaultsConfig, FunctionConfig, and ResolvedFunctionConfig. Add SetupMode enum (PerFunction, PerExecution). Add type_name: Option<String> to ParamInfo. Extend resolve_function_config_with_inputs to resolve setup files, param_generators, and type_generators. Extend merge_configs for the new generators map. Unit tests for all new config parsing and resolution.

title: High-fidelity compositional mock generation
description: When function A calls function B, and B's behavior map is available, generate mocks for B that are input-conditional decision trees: 'when called with X return Y, when called with Z throw'. Current to_mock_config() replays flat return values. Needs to capture: input-conditional branching, ordering constraints (A calls B then C), error modes (what happens when dependency fails). Builds on enriched behavior maps and scan_orchestrator's existing mock-injection architecture.

title: Support user-provided candidate inputs via .shatter/ convention
description:
## Problem

Shatter currently generates all inputs via random seed generation (biased boundary values) and Z3 constraint solving. There is no way for users to provide domain-specific candidate inputs that reflect real-world usage patterns, known edge cases, or regression-triggering values. Additionally, execution limits (max_iterations, timeout, etc.) are global — users cannot tune them per-function, even though complex functions may need deeper exploration while simple ones can stop early.

## Proposed Convention

### Directory Discovery (Hierarchical)

`.shatter/` directories can appear at any level in the source tree. Shatter walks upward from each target file to find the nearest `.shatter/` directory, then continues to the project root, merging configuration with nearest-wins precedence:

```
project-root/
  .shatter/                     # project-wide defaults
    config.yaml
    inputs/global/candidates.json
  packages/
    auth/
      .shatter/                 # auth-specific overrides
        config.yaml             # merges over root config
        inputs/
          validateToken/candidates.json
      src/
        auth.ts
    utils/
      .shatter/                 # utils-specific overrides
        config.yaml
      src/
        helpers.ts
```

Resolution order (most specific wins):
1. Nearest `.shatter/config.yaml` function-level overrides
2. Nearest `.shatter/config.yaml` defaults
3. Parent `.shatter/config.yaml` function-level overrides
4. Parent `.shatter/config.yaml` defaults
5. ... up to project root
6. CLI flags
7. Built-in defaults

This mirrors how tools like `.eslintrc`, `.gitignore`, and `tsconfig.json` work — configuration lives close to the code it governs, with inheritance up the tree.

### Directory Layout

```
.shatter/
  inputs/
    <function-or-module>/     # scoped by target
      candidates.json         # array of candidate input sets
    global/
      candidates.json         # applies to all matching signatures
  config.yaml                 # configuration for this subtree
```

### Input Format

Each candidate is a JSON array of argument values matching the target function's parameter list:

```json
[
  { "args": [42, "hello"], "label": "typical usage" },
  { "args": [-1, ""], "label": "edge: negative with empty" },
  { "args": [0, null], "label": "regression: issue-123" }
]
```

### Per-Function Execution Limits & Parameters

`config.yaml` supports function-level overrides. Function matching uses `<file>:<function>` patterns with glob support at both the file and function level:

```yaml
# Defaults for everything under this .shatter/'s subtree.
# Names mirror CLI flags exactly (see "CLI/Config Parallelism" below).
defaults:
  max_iterations: 100
  timeout: 60
  scope: ./shatter.scope.yaml
  cache_dir: .shatter/cache
  no_cache: false
  analyze_only: false
  show_clusters: false

# Per-function overrides.
#
# Keys are <file-glob>:<function-glob> patterns. Either side can be a glob
# or omitted. Examples:
#
#   "src/auth.ts:validateToken"      — exact file, exact function
#   "src/auth.ts:*"                  — all functions in one file
#   "src/auth.ts"                    — same as above (":*" is implicit)
#   "*:validateToken"                — function name in any file
#   "src/**/*.ts:process*"           — file glob + function prefix glob
#   "src/generated/**"               — skip all functions in matching files
#
# When multiple patterns match a function, the most specific match wins
# (exact > single-glob > double-glob), with later entries breaking ties.
functions:
  "src/auth.ts:validateToken":
    max_iterations: 500       # complex function, explore deeper
    timeout: 120
    inputs: ./inputs/validateToken/candidates.json

  "src/auth.ts":              # all functions in this file
    max_iterations: 200
    timeout: 90

  "*:computeStats":           # this function name in any file
    max_iterations: 300

  "src/utils/**":             # all functions in all utils files
    max_iterations: 20
    timeout: 15

  "src/generated/**":
    skip: true                # skip entirely
```

### CLI / Config Parallelism

Config key names MUST match CLI flag names exactly (minus the `--` prefix), using snake_case. Every CLI flag that controls execution behavior should be settable in config.yaml, and vice versa. This is a project-wide convention — see the documentation update below.

Current CLI flags and their config.yaml equivalents:

| CLI flag | config.yaml key | Type | Default | Applies to |
|----------|----------------|------|---------|------------|
| `--max-iterations` | `max_iterations` | integer | 100 (explore), 50 (run) | explore, scan, run, export-tests |
| `--timeout` | `timeout` | integer (seconds) | 60 (explore), 300 (run) | explore, scan, run, export-tests |
| `--scope` | `scope` | path | none | explore, scan, export-tests |
| `--cache-dir` | `cache_dir` | path | .shatter/cache | explore |
| `--no-cache` | `no_cache` | boolean | false | explore, scan |
| `--analyze-only` | `analyze_only` | boolean | false | explore, scan, run |
| `--show-clusters` | `show_clusters` | boolean | false | explore |
| (new) `--inputs` | `inputs` | path | none | explore, scan, run |
| (new) `--config` | `config` | path | auto-discovered | all |
| n/a | `skip` | boolean | false | functions block only |

Note: `--config` is CLI-only (it points to the config file itself). `skip` is config-only (the CLI uses target selection instead).

### Integration Points

1. **Orchestrator worklist**: User-provided candidates enter the worklist as a new `InputSource::UserProvided` with highest priority (above Z3Solved)
2. **ExploreConfig resolution**: Hierarchical config merge — nearest `.shatter/` wins, then parents, then CLI flags, then built-in defaults
3. **Discovery**: `shatter run` walks the directory tree collecting `.shatter/` directories; `shatter explore` finds the nearest one from the target file
4. **CLI**: `shatter explore --inputs <path>` for explicit input file; `--config <path>` for explicit config (bypasses discovery)
5. **Scope integration**: Function `skip` in config.yaml complements shatter.scope.yaml (file-level scope vs function-level control)

## Documentation Update

Add to CLAUDE.md under a new "CLI / Config Parallelism" section:

> When adding a CLI flag that controls execution behavior, ensure it is also settable in `.shatter/config.yaml` under the same snake_case name. When adding a config.yaml key, ensure there is a corresponding CLI flag (unless the setting is config-only by nature, like `skip`). The CLI flag is the canonical name; config.yaml mirrors it.

This ensures the two surfaces never diverge as new parameters are added.

## Out of Scope (for now)

- Automatic corpus management (saving interesting inputs back to .shatter/inputs/)
- Input format validation against TypeInfo
- IDE/editor integration for authoring inputs
- Per-function solver configuration (timeout, tactics)
- Config inheritance across git submodules

title: LLM adapter: Anthropic (Claude)
description:
Implement a SeedOracle adapter for the Anthropic API (Claude Sonnet, Claude Haiku, etc.).

- Use the Anthropic messages API with tool_use for structured output
- Support API key via SHATTER_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var or config file
- Support model selection via llm.anthropic.model (default: claude-sonnet-4-6)
- Map generic SeedOracle parameters (temperature, max_tokens, timeout) to Anthropic API fields
- Handle rate limiting with exponential backoff
- Handle API errors gracefully (auth failures, quota exceeded, overloaded)

title: Rust frontend: static analyzer using syn
description: Implement the Analyze command handler using the syn crate to parse Rust source files. Extract: (1) function signatures with parameter names and types mapped to TypeInfo (i32/i64→Int, f32/f64→Float, String/&str→Str, bool→Bool, Vec<T>→Array, structs→Object, Option<T>→Nullable, Result<T,E>→Union, enums→tagged unions), (2) branch locations from if/else, match arms, while/for/loop, and ? operator, (3) branch conditions as SymExpr trees (comparisons, boolean ops, pattern matches), (4) external dependencies (function calls to other modules/crates). Key challenge: Rust's rich type system and pattern matching need careful mapping. syn gives us AST-level info without type inference — document limitations vs rustc API approach.

title: shatter.config.json project-level configuration
description: Support a shatter.config.json in the project root for persistent scan configuration: default include/exclude patterns, mock overrides per module/function, timeout defaults, output preferences. CLI flags override config file values. Document the schema in README.

title: Implement symbolic instrumentation in TypeScript frontend
description: Implement code instrumentation in shatter-ts that rewrites the target function to inject __branch() callbacks at every branch point and __sym() wrappers around symbolic values. The instrumented code must: (1) record which branches are taken with their conditions, (2) capture symbolic path constraints as SymExpr trees, (3) be executable via Node.js worker thread. This connects the TS frontend to the concolic loop.
close_reason: Implemented symbolic instrumentation: instrumentor wraps branch conditions with __shatter_branch() callbacks capturing SymExpr constraints, executor collects BranchDecision records during instrumented execution, handlers wire up instrument/execute pipeline. Fixed TS protocol types to match Rust serde format. 92 tests passing.

title: CLI: detect .rs files and spawn shatter-rust frontend
description: Update shatter-cli to detect .rs file extensions and spawn the shatter-rust binary as the frontend subprocess. Follow the same pattern as TypeScript (.ts → node shatter-ts) and Go (.go → shatter-go binary). Add a --frontend=rust flag override. Update the frontend resolution logic in shatter-cli/src/main.rs or embedded_frontend.rs. The shatter-rust binary should be discoverable via PATH or embedded (like shatter-ts bundle).

title: Scan orchestrator: drive exploration in dependency order
description: Build the scan orchestrator in shatter-core that: (1) takes the topologically-sorted exploration plan, (2) spawns N frontend subprocesses (multi-process parallelism, default=CPU count), (3) assigns functions to workers in dependency order, (4) collects results and feeds behavior maps forward to the next layer, (5) handles timeouts and errors per-function without aborting the whole scan. CLI flag: --parallelism N and --timeout-per-fn SECONDS (default 30).

title: Rust frontend: protocol handler
description: Implement the JSON-over-stdio protocol handler in shatter-rust. Reuse or mirror the types from shatter-core/src/protocol.rs (Request, Response, Command, ResponseResult). Handle all command variants: Handshake, Analyze, Instrument, Execute, Shutdown. Dispatch to stub handlers that return NotImplemented errors initially. Include round-trip serialization tests for all message types, matching the pattern in shatter-ts and shatter-go protocol tests.

title: Topological ordering for bottom-up exploration
description: Given the call graph, produce an exploration order: leaf functions (no internal deps) first, then their callers, etc. Functions in cycles are grouped and explored together. Each layer can be explored in parallel since functions in the same layer are independent. Output: ordered list of exploration batches.

title: Enrich behavior maps with dependency I/O traces
description: Extend BehaviorMap to capture not just input→output pairs but the full dependency interaction trace for each execution: sequence of external calls made (with arguments and return values), side effects observed (DB writes, file I/O, network), ordering constraints (A called B then C). The ExternalCall and SideEffect fields exist in the protocol but are not meaningfully used in behavior map construction or mock generation. This enrichment is prerequisite for realistic mock generation — mocks need to be input-conditional decision trees, not flat replay of return values.

title: Build intra-project call graph from analyze results
description: Using the function registry from batch analysis, construct a directed call graph where edges represent function A calls function B (both within the project). Resolve imports/requires to map dependency names to concrete functions. Detect cycles and mark them. Output: adjacency list, topological layers, and cycle groups.

title: Detect self-recursive functions and identify base-case branches
description:
Add analysis to distinguish base-case branches from recursive branches within a function.

## Context

When analyzing a function f, the frontend reports branches and which functions each branch calls. For recursive functions, some branches call f (recursive cases) and some don't (base cases). This structural property is detectable from the call graph + branch analysis without running the function.

## Implementation

1. In CallGraph, detect self-edges (f calls f) and mark those functions as self-recursive instead of rejecting the entire graph as cyclic.
2. Modify test_order() to handle self-recursive functions: remove the self-edge, include the function in topological order, and annotate it as self-recursive.
3. Add a BranchClassification enum or similar to distinguish base-case branches (no self-call) from recursive branches (contains self-call) for self-recursive functions.
4. Expose this classification through the behavior module so downstream code can prioritize base-case branch exploration.

## Key files

- shatter-core/src/behavior.rs — CallGraph, test_order(), BehaviorMap
- shatter-core/src/protocol/types.rs — FunctionAnalysis, BranchInfo

## Tests

- Self-recursive function (e.g. factorial) is not rejected as a cycle; test_order() returns it with a self-recursive annotation
- Branches of a self-recursive function are correctly classified as base-case vs recursive
- Mutual recursion (a→b→a) is still detected as a cycle (handled separately)
close_reason: Implemented self-recursive function detection in CallGraph. Self-edges are stored separately, test_order() returns TestOrderEntry with is_self_recursive annotation, mutual recursion still detected as cycle.

title: Behavioral equivalence classes for path grouping
description: Replace return-value-based path deduplication with branch-path-based behavioral equivalence classes. Executions that follow the same branch sequence belong to the same class, regardless of output value. Within each class: pick the simplest concrete example as the canonical representative, derive common preconditions from all inputs in the class. This fixes the fundamental issue in str-798 (every distinct return value treated as unique path) at the conceptual level and is prerequisite for meaningful specification generation.

title: Regression detection via specification diff
description: The diff CLI command exists but needs to produce actionable output by comparing specifications, not raw traces. When a function's behavior changes between versions, report: (1) new behavioral paths added, (2) existing paths removed, (3) preconditions that changed, (4) postconditions that changed (e.g. 'Path 3 no longer throws ValidationError — now returns null'), (5) properties that no longer hold. This is the core regression detection use case — diffing specs is what makes behavior maps useful for CI.

title: Extract branches from Go AST in analyzer
description: The Go analyzer (shatter-go/protocol/analyzer.go) needs to extract branches from if/else, switch/case, and select statements using go/ast. Each branch needs: id, line number, condition expression as string. Parallel to the TS branch extraction work.

title: E2E concolic test on real TS function
description: Add an integration test that runs the FULL concolic pipeline on a real TypeScript example function (e.g. classifyNumber): analyze → instrument → explore with concolic orchestrator → Z3 solve → verify that Z3 discovers a branch that random alone cannot. This is the single most important validation that the core value proposition works end-to-end. Must use the real TS frontend subprocess, not a mock.

title: Protocol: Add Rust frontend types + stub handlers for new commands
description: Add protocol types for Setup, Teardown, Generate commands and responses in shatter-rust/src/protocol.rs and handler.rs. Implement stub handlers that return 'not yet implemented' error responses. Update tests.

title: Rust target example functions in examples/rust/
description: Create examples/rust/src/ with a set of known-answer Rust functions for integration testing. Include: (1) simple numeric branching (if x < 0 / x > 100), (2) string comparison (match on &str), (3) enum/match exhaustiveness (Result, Option, custom enums), (4) struct field access with nested conditions, (5) loop with early return, (6) functions with external dependencies (trait-based for mockability), (7) functions using ? operator for error propagation. Each function should have a comment documenting all reachable branches and the inputs that trigger them. These serve as the regression test suite for the Rust frontend.

title: Custom LLM adapter framework
description:
Provide an adapter framework for integrating Shatter with any LLM, including private cloud deployments and locally-hosted models.

## HTTP adapter (remote custom LLMs)
- Generic HTTP POST adapter configurable via config file
- Configurable endpoint URL (llm.custom.url), headers, auth scheme
- Configurable request/response JSON path mappings (where to place the prompt, where to read the response)
- Support for Bearer token, API key header, or no auth
- Timeout, retry, and backoff settings

## Local model adapter (host-resident LLMs)
- Support for OpenAI-compatible local servers (llama.cpp, ollama, vLLM, etc.) via the same HTTP adapter with localhost URLs
- Optional subprocess management: start/stop a local model server as a child process
  - llm.local.command — command to launch the server (e.g., 'ollama serve')
  - llm.local.model — model to load (e.g., 'llama3.2')
  - llm.local.port — port to connect on (default: 11434 for ollama)
  - llm.local.startup_timeout_seconds — time to wait for server readiness (default: 60)
  - Health check endpoint polling before sending requests

## Adapter registration
- Adapters register by name in config (llm.adapter = 'openai' | 'anthropic' | 'google' | 'custom' | 'local')
- Custom adapters selected via llm.adapter = 'custom' with llm.custom.* config block
- Local adapters selected via llm.adapter = 'local' with llm.local.* config block
- All adapters implement the same SeedOracle trait from the core LLM integration issue

title: Add BehaviorMap disk cache in shatter-core
description: BehaviorMaps are currently in-memory only. Add a BehaviorMapCache abstraction that can serialize/deserialize BehaviorMaps to a directory on disk, keyed by function_id. Default cache location should be .shatter/cache/ relative to the target project root. Cache should be the default behavior (not opt-in). The cache module should handle: writing a BehaviorMap to disk after it's built from execution records, loading a cached BehaviorMap if one exists for a function_id, invalidating/overwriting stale entries when new records are collected. File format: JSON, one file per function (e.g. .shatter/cache/getDiscount.json). Additionally, support a --cache-dir <path> CLI argument and SHATTER_CACHE_DIR environment variable to override the default cache directory. The CLI flag takes precedence over the env var, which takes precedence over the default. The path can be absolute (e.g. /tmp/shatter-cache) or relative to CWD.

title: Support mutual recursion in compositional testing
description:
Extend recursive function support to handle mutually recursive function groups (e.g. is_even/is_odd, parser combinators).

## Context

Self-recursion (f calls f) is handled by iterative deepening with self-call mocking. Mutual recursion (f calls g, g calls f) requires bootstrapping the entire cycle simultaneously.

## Strategy

1. Detect strongly connected components (SCCs) in the call graph. Each SCC with >1 node is a mutual recursion group.
2. For each function in the group, identify base-case branches (branches that don't call any other function in the group).
3. Bootstrap: solve for base-case inputs across ALL functions in the group simultaneously, building initial BehaviorMaps for each.
4. Iterative deepening: at each depth, mock cross-calls within the group using BehaviorMaps from depth ≤ k. Explore all functions in the group at each depth.
5. The group's BehaviorMaps grow together. Stop when coverage stabilizes across all functions or max depth is reached.

## Implementation

1. Replace simple cycle detection in CallGraph with SCC detection (Tarjan's or Kosaraju's algorithm).
2. Single-node SCCs with a self-edge → self-recursive (handled by existing iterative deepening).
3. Multi-node SCCs → mutual recursion groups. Process as a unit in test_order().
4. Extend the orchestrator's deepening loop to handle groups: at each depth, explore each function in the group with mocks for all other functions in the group.

## Example: is_even(n) / is_odd(n)

- Base cases: is_even(0)→true, is_odd(0)→false
- Depth 1: is_even(1) calls is_odd(0)→false from mock → is_even(1)→false; is_odd(1) calls is_even(0)→true from mock → is_odd(1)→true
- Depth 2: is_even(2) calls is_odd(1)→true → is_even(2)→true; etc.

## Scope

This is lower priority than self-recursion support since mutual recursion is less common in practice. Most mutual recursion involves small cycles (2-3 functions). Large cycles may need special handling or manual annotation.

## Key files

- shatter-core/src/behavior.rs — CallGraph, test_order()
- shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs — explore loop

## Tests

- is_even/is_odd: mutual recursion detected, base cases found, BehaviorMaps built correctly
- Three-function cycle: A→B→C→A, all base cases found
- Mixed graph: some functions self-recursive, some mutually recursive, some neither — all handled correctly
close_reason: Implemented mutual recursion support: Tarjan's SCC algorithm in CallGraph, MutualGroup variant in TestOrderEntry, explore_mutual_group() for group-level iterative deepening.

title: Rust frontend: executor (compile + run)
description: Implement the Execute command handler using a subprocess compilation approach: (1) Write instrumented source to a temp directory with a Cargo.toml or as a single-file rustc compilation, (2) Compile using rustc or cargo build, (3) Run the compiled binary with JSON-serialized inputs on stdin, (4) Parse the output: return value, thrown error (panic), branch path taken, lines executed, path constraints as SymExpr, external call records, performance metrics. The compiled binary needs a main() harness that deserializes inputs, calls the target function, and serializes the ExecuteResult. Handle compilation errors gracefully (return protocol Error). Handle panics (catch_unwind or subprocess exit code). Consider caching compiled binaries when source hasn't changed.

title: Rust type system: handle ownership, lifetimes, and generics in analysis
description: Extend the Rust analyzer to handle Rust-specific type system features that don't exist in TS/Go: (1) References and borrowing — &T, &mut T should map to the underlying TypeInfo of T, (2) Lifetimes — can be ignored for symbolic analysis since they don't affect runtime behavior, (3) Generics — monomorphize or treat as Unknown when type params aren't concrete, (4) Trait bounds — extract callable methods from trait constraints, (5) Enums with data (tagged unions) — map to Union with discriminant, (6) Pattern matching exhaustiveness — leverage match arms as branch enumeration. This is the hardest part of Rust support — the type system is significantly richer than TS or Go.

title: Iterative deepening via self-call mocking
description:
Implement the core recursive function testing strategy: iteratively build a BehaviorMap by mocking self-calls at increasing depth.

## Context

Once base-case inputs are known (from branch analysis or small-value probing), we can test recursive functions compositionally by treating self-calls the same way we treat calls to other functions — mock them using the BehaviorMap built so far.

## Algorithm

1. Start with the base-case BehaviorMap (depth 0): inputs that don't trigger any recursive call.
2. For depth k+1:
   a. Configure mocks for self-calls using the BehaviorMap from depth ≤ k
   b. Explore the function — the solver targets recursive branches
   c. When a recursive branch is taken, the self-call is intercepted and returns a mocked result from the existing BehaviorMap
   d. New input→output pairs discovered at this depth are added to the BehaviorMap
3. Repeat until: coverage stabilizes (no new branches discovered), or max depth reached.
4. The final BehaviorMap is used by callers of this function, same as any other function.

## Implementation

1. Add a max_recursion_depth field to ExploreConfig (default: 10).
2. In the orchestrator, when processing a self-recursive function:
   a. Run base-case exploration first (constraint solve for non-recursive branches + probing)
   b. Enter the iterative deepening loop
   c. At each depth, create a MockConfig from the current BehaviorMap and configure it for self-calls
   d. Track coverage across depths; stop when no new behaviors are found
3. The BehaviorMap grows incrementally. Each depth adds new entries.

## Example: factorial(n)

- Depth 0: base case n≤1 → BehaviorMap = {0→1, 1→1}
- Depth 1: mock factorial(n-1) using depth-0 map → try n=2, gets factorial(1)=1 from mock → BehaviorMap adds {2→2}
- Depth 2: try n=3, gets factorial(2)=2 from mock → BehaviorMap adds {3→6}
- Coverage stabilizes (both branches covered at depth 1), but we continue to configured max depth for richer behavior map.

## Key files

- shatter-core/src/orchestrator.rs — ExploreConfig, explore loop
- shatter-core/src/behavior.rs — BehaviorMap, MockConfig, CompositeResult

## Tests

- factorial(n): iterative deepening builds correct BehaviorMap up to configured depth
- fibonacci(n): two recursive calls per invocation, BehaviorMap grows correctly
- Coverage stabilization: deepening stops when no new branches are found
- Max depth cutoff: deepening stops at configured limit even if new behaviors exist
close_reason: Implemented iterative deepening via self-call mocking in recursive.rs. explore_recursive() probes for base cases, then iteratively deepens by mocking self-calls with BehaviorMap, stopping when coverage stabilizes or max_depth reached.

title: Explore bounded symbolic execution for array/collection parameters
description:
**This is a future exploration item, not a gap in current functionality.** The current engine already handles arrays through its general concolic loop — it executes concretely, collects per-element branch decisions, negates them with Z3, and falls back to fuzzing for unsolvable constraints. Execution budgets and plateau detection prevent runaway exploration. This works.

The question is whether we can do *better* with array-aware strategies. Sequential processing of collection elements causes exponential path explosion (2^n paths for n elements with one branch each), and the engine currently has no special knowledge that it's exploring array contents vs any other parameter. A smarter approach could focus exploration budget more effectively.

Areas to investigate:

1. **Bounded unrolling** — Recognize array parameters and deliberately generate inputs at lengths 0, 1, 2, 3 rather than random 0-5. Most real bugs appear at small sizes. Spend solving budget on per-element branches at those sizes.

2. **Early-exit path detection** — Identify find/some/indexOf patterns where interesting paths are linear (found at index k, not found) rather than exponential.

3. **Quantified constraints** — Evaluate whether Z3 forall/exists quantifiers are practical for simple array predicates like 'exists element > 0'.

4. **Loop summarization** — Research feasibility of inferring loop invariants for common patterns (filter, map, reduce with thresholds).

5. **Coverage-directed array mutation** — AFL-style splice/insert/delete mutations for array contents, complementing the existing fuzzing fallback.

Prerequisite: overlay_solved_values would need to reconstruct nested JSON from solved flat values for some of these approaches.

title: Extract branches from TypeScript AST in analyzer
description: The TS analyzer (shatter-ts/src/analyzer.ts) currently returns an empty branches array. Implement branch extraction from if/else, switch/case, ternary, and logical &&/|| expressions using the TypeScript Compiler API. Each branch needs: id, line number, condition expression as string. This is a prerequisite for concolic execution — without branch info, the solver has nothing to target.

title: Staged pipeline: observe → analyze → solve → specify

Stage 2 (Analyze): Build behavior map, identify uncovered branches, generate draft spec. Stage 3 (Solve): For uncovered branches, use Z3 to find triggering inputs, feed back to Stage 1. Stage 4 (Specify): Final specification with full coverage and derived properties. Each stage is independently useful and testable. Users get value from Stage 1+2 alone. This is an architectural refactor, not a rewrite — the pieces exist but are coupled.

Each generated test calls the function with a discovered input and asserts the observed output. Tests serve as regression snapshots of discovered behavior.
